coredata.dc:title: Alginate based 3D micro-scaffolds mimicking tumor architecture as in vitro cell culture platform
coredata.dc:creator: De, Shreemoyee
Joshi, Akshay
Tripathi, Dinesh M.
Kaur, Savneet
Singh, Neetu
coredata.dc:description: A micron scale alginate based 3D platform embedded with a carbon dot pH sensor, that enables continuous growth monitoring of encapsulated cells in real time is reported. The alginate based 3D micro-scaffold closely mimics a tumor microenvironment by providing a spatial demarcation and making it possible to encapsulate different cells in close proximity. The micro-scaffold contains carbon dot based nanosensors that enable real time monitoring of pH change in the tumor microenvironment avoiding the need for end-point assays for studying cellular growth. The micro-scaffolds have heterogeneous architecture and a hypoxic core region can be observed in as less as 96 h of culture. In this completely synthetic platform, there also exist the flexibility of artificially modifying the porosity of the micro-scaffold as per the requirement of the studies where a denser ECM mimic is required. The micro-scaffolds were conducive for cell growth as suggested by the enhanced functional profile of hepatocellular carcinoma cells and positively influence the genetic expression of the cell specific markers. Additionally, similar to a 3D tumor, non-homogeneous diffusion of molecules is also observed making this an ideal platform for cancer modelling and drug screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3D cell culture
tumor model
alginate based micro-scaffolds

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Designing and characterization of core-shell micro-scaffolds
Genetic expression and functionality of encapsulated cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institut de Recherche pour le Développement

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from Indian Institute of Technology Delhi. We are grateful to CRF, IITD for SEM facility and NRF, IITD for FTIR and CBME, IITD for RTPCR facility. We acknowledge Dr. Mayank Singh's lab for gifting us the cell line U2OS. We also acknowledge Prof. Harpal Sir's lab and Ms. Neha, CBME for confocal imaging. We acknowledge IITD FIRP project (MI0226) sponsored by MHRD, Govt. of India and IRD, IITD.

We acknowledge financial support from Indian Institute of Technology Delhi . We are grateful to CRF, IITD for SEM facility and NRF, IITD for FTIR and CBME, IITD for RTPCR facility. We acknowledge Dr. Mayank Singh's lab for gifting us the cell line U2OS. We also acknowledge Prof. Harpal Sir's lab and Ms. Neha, CBME for confocal imaging. We acknowledge IITD FIRP project (MI0226) sponsored by MHRD, Govt. of India and IRD, IITD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of coaxial electrospray system for generating microcapsule with various compositions of core and shell in single step using two aqueous fluids. (b) FTIR spectra for core and shell of the micro-scaffold. (c) SEM micrographs for core-shell system showing pores at 1000× magnification. (d) The core and shell shown by brightfield and fluorescent imaging of micro-scaffold developed with a green fluorescent acrylate dye in the core. (e) SEM micrographs showing the pore size variation with varying crosslinking density (PEGDA: 3% and 8% in shell). (Scale bar for SEM images: 20 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Microscopic fluorescent imaging of the encapsulated dye labeled cells (MG63) at 2.5× magnification; (a)-(c) show the spatial arrangement of the cells in the microsphere (Cellular dyes used, Green- CMFDA dye; Red- CM-Dil). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Growth curves. (a) The change in pH was plotted as a ratio of emission intensities at 450 nm (blue) and 550 nm (green) for the various cell lines for five days. In addition, Alamar blue fluorescence intensity reading was recorded and plotted for various cell lines over 5 days. (b) The growth curves for osteosarcoma cell lines were compared using pH sensitive carbon dots. (c) Green fluorescence observed due to the fluorogenic dye, Hypoxia-IT, due to hypoxic regions in the micro-scaffold (imaged by bright field) at (i) 48 h and (ii) 96 h at 4× magnification. Green fluorescent intensity line graphs were generated for the respective time points. The growth curves (a) and (b) are an average of 3 biological replicate having 3 technical replicate each. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Albumin production by culture of Huh-7 cells in tissue culture plates (2D) and encapsulated in alginate micro-scaffolds (3D) (b) Gene expression analysis of markers for hepatic progenitor cells using qPCR (c) The genotypic expression of EMT markers in osteosarcoma cells (MG-63) using qPCR. The graphs plot are an average of 3 biological replicate with 3 technical replicate each. (Significant difference, p < 0.05).

(a) Doxorubicin (Dox) diffusion and its effect on encapsulated cells in the 3D micro-scaffolds. After 30 min of Dox exposure, the encapsulated cells were stained with Calcein AM (live cell stain) to observe the toxicity effect of Dox across the depth of the micro-scaffold by confocal laser scanning microscopy. The presence of doxorubicin was confirmed by its red fluorescence. (b) Alamar blue assay for microscaffolds and tissue culture plates on MG-63 cells. Images are captured at 10× magnification. The graph plot is an average of 2 biological replicate and 3 technical replicate each. Scale bar: 200 μm; (One way ANOVA, * p < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Alginate based 3D micro-scaffolds mimicking tumor architecture as in vitro cell culture platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi-110016, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Molecular and Cellular Medicine, Institute of Liver and Biliary Sciences, D1, Vasant Kunj Marg, New Delhi, Delhi 110070, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Molecular and Cellular Medicine
Institute of Liver and Biliary Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Molecular and Cellular Medicine, Institute of Liver and Biliary Sciences, D1, Vasant Kunj Marg, New Delhi, Delhi 110070

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomedical Engineering Unit, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Ansari Nagar, New Delhi 110029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: All India Institute of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomedical Engineering Unit, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Ansari Nagar, New Delhi-110029, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A micron scale alginate based 3D platform embedded with a carbon dot pH sensor, that enables continuous growth monitoring of encapsulated cells in real time is reported. The alginate based 3D micro-scaffold closely mimics a tumor microenvironment by providing a spatial demarcation and making it possible to encapsulate different cells in close proximity. The micro-scaffold contains carbon dot based nanosensors that enable real time monitoring of pH change in the tumor microenvironment avoiding the need for end-point assays for studying cellular growth. The micro-scaffolds have heterogeneous architecture and a hypoxic core region can be observed in as less as 96 h of culture. In this completely synthetic platform, there also exist the flexibility of artificially modifying the porosity of the micro-scaffold as per the requirement of the studies where a denser ECM mimic is required. The micro-scaffolds were conducive for cell growth as suggested by the enhanced functional profile of hepatocellular carcinoma cells and positively influence the genetic expression of the cell specific markers. Additionally, similar to a 3D tumor, non-homogeneous diffusion of molecules is also observed making this an ideal platform for cancer modelling and drug screening.

ce:section.ce:para: In vitro disease models are the most convenient approach for understanding biological processes, their mechanisms and factors affecting it. Such models also form a low cost drug screening platform with the benefit of genetic modifications, small sample sizes and easy analysis [1]. However, a disparity has been observed between studies performed in vitro and in in vivo animal models. Only approximately 10% of the drugs that enter into the phase I clinical trial do successfully proceed into phase III of the trials. One of the primary reasons for this disparity can be attributed to the two dimensional (2D) cell culture techniques on which most of the studies rely. [2]. Cells in situ are in three dimensional architecture (3D), they are always surrounded by other cells and extracellular matrix components [3]. The monolayer culturing of cells in 2D change their expression profiles, concentration and arrangement of various surface receptors [4]. These changes lead to altered biology and hence may not unravel the exact same biological processes that happens in vivo [5]. 2D culturing also lacks the heterogeneity of the in vivo condition. Tumor architecture as observed in vivo, consists of a mixture of cells consisting of the necrotic, hypoxic core followed by non-proliferating cells and proliferating cells in the end. The cancer cell interactions with normal cells can be suppressive in the initial phases but eventually they are converted into interactions that promote the cancer progression. Thus, tumor development is dependent both on cell-cell and cell-substrate interaction [6]. Alternative approaches to understand these complexities of in vivo processes better are animal models designed for various disease conditions. These models do offer an actual environment but they are still not the ideal systems as they are from different species, are quite expensive and require skilled labor [7]. Hence, to bridge the gap between the two extremes various forthcoming 3D platforms are being developed as disease models [8,9].

3D platforms offer a better opportunity to mimic the tumor microenvironment as well as facilitate genetic manipulation, biochemical analysis and imaging, resulting in better understanding of the biological processes. An ideal 3D model would include cell-to-cell and cell-ECM interactions, tissue specific stiffness, oxygen, nutrient and metabolic waste gradients, no toxicity with properties to proliferate cell adhesion, proliferation and migration [10]. In order to mimic the in vivo like niche it is important to have multiple cell types arranged in a region specific manner to study various intercellular signaling pathways. Thus, it is important to incorporate spatial control in encapsulating cells in 3D in vitro models to develop a platform that can truly mimic the in vivo niche microenvironment. Such in vitro platforms not only allow drug screening on patient specific tumoroids but also aid in fundamental understanding of tumor progression and various cell-cell and cell-ECM interactions in tumors [11]. Thus, these models are becoming indispensable for cancer research.

The 3D platforms established till date lack control over size and shape and most importantly they lack control over spatial distribution of cells. Spheroid formation has been done by various methods such as spinner flask, gyratory rotation systems and traditional methods like hanging drop method using ultra low attachment plates. The limitation is spheroid culture is they allow co-culturing of cells but all in random locations without providing any control over their spatial distribution and make studying the effect of one cell on another systematically very difficult and inconvenient in 3D arrangement [12]. Classical 3D culture models, using bioreactors with the latest modification of rotating wall vessel technique does not allow miniaturization, reproducible yield as well as have the drawback of not being able to maintain long term cell culture [8,9,13]. Newer microfluidic techniques provide advantages of being fast, high-throughput and size controllable [14]. In addition, growing cells in micron sized scaffolds also makes it easier and faster to observe biological process due to lower volumes.

In this study, we have developed an alginate based simple platform via microfluidics that closely mimics the tumor architecture. It provides a favorable cell growth substrate along with providing the much needed spatial control for growing co-cultures of various cell lines in different regions, which benefits in mimicking the heterogeneous population of tumors. It also enables real-time monitoring of the micro environmental changes in the ECM along with cellular growth in an “in vivo” like environment. The real time monitoring removes the need of additional end-point assays for monitoring cellular growth as simply monitoring of the pH changes in the microenvironment, which correlates with cellular proliferation, can give information about the growth of the encapsulated cells. The pH change is recorded by incorporating carbon dot based nano-sensors along with the encapsulated cells. The micron sized platform has allowed us to achieve the hypoxic core region of a tumor in just four days in comparison to about two weeks when tumoroids are formed by prevailing methods. The platform has been shown to maintain the cellular functionality of the encapsulated hepatocellular carcinoma cells. It also showed better expression profile for cellular specific marker as well as cell migratory markers in the encapsulated cells. Thus, this platform gives the flexibility to modulate its properties according to the requirement of the model to be designed. It provides spatial control as well as better the cellular characteristics to yield reliable results in drug screening.

Sodium alginate (medium viscosity) and PEGDA (Mol. Wt. 575) was purchased from Sigma Life Sciences. Gelatin, Type A was purchased from MP Biomedicals, India. CellTracker™ Green CMFDA Dye, CellTracker™ CM-DiI Dye, Calcein AM, Image-iT™ Green Hypoxia Reagent, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), Antibiotic-Antimycotic solution, heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's Phosphate-buffered Saline (DPBS) powder, N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N-2-ethane sulfonic acid (HEPES), Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS), TrypLE™ Express Enzyme (1X), ethylenediamine from ThermoFisher Scientific. UV LED (365 nm) with 1200 mW flux output at 2.7 W power dissipation was purchased from Mouser Electronics, USA. A549, MG-63, MDA-MB231, HuH-7, U2OS and L929 cell lines were procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India.

Sodium alginate and gelatin powder were weighed and UV sterilized inside the laminar hood. The powder mix was allowed to dissolve in autoclaved PBS on a stirrer. The mixing of the solutions were carried out inside the laminar air flow followed by microgel preparation, which was also done inside the biosafety cabinet. The core fluid consisted of 3% (w/v) gelatin and 1% (w/v) alginate. The shell fluid consisted of 2% gelatin and 1% alginate or PEGDA (3% (w/v) or 8% (w/v)).

Coaxial needle was ordered from ramé hart instrument company. The needle consisted of concentric inner (14 g) and outer (22 g) lumens. Under an open electric field from a voltage generator, concentric drops of the two coaxial fluids at the needle tip were broken up into micro-drops and sprayed into the gelling bath containing 100 mM calcium chloride solution to instantly gel alginate in the shell fluid before the two fluids got mixed. The shell solution containing PEGDA was added with photo-initiator 0.1% Irgacure 2959. These droplets were photo polymerized by UV irradiation for 30 s.

Carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using Agaricus bisporus as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source in deionized water. Lyophilized Agaricus bisporus powder (0.6 g) and 3 ml of 1 M ethylenediamine were dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The solution was hydrothermally heated at 160 °C in a teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for 12 h. The autoclave was kept for cooling by itself at room temperature till it reached 37 °C. The dark yellow solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min to remove the debris left after the reaction. The supernatant was collected and dialysis was setup. Dialysis was done for 3 days under constant stirring at room temperature using a 3.5 kDa cutoff dialysis tubing against deionized water. The solution containing carbon dots were stored at 4 °C until further use.

Overnight grown MG-63 cells were trypsinized, with a cell count of 106 cells per ml was suspended in the core or shell solution as per the requirement of the experiment. The spatially separated regions were shown by labeling the cells with different cell tracker dyes. The core cells were stained with green CMFDA dye and cell in shell were stained with red CM-DIL dye. The cells were incubated at 37 °C for 5 min with the dyes. They were then mixed with the solution. The solution was pipetted up and down to achieve a uniform suspension. The uniformly suspended solution was then loaded to the coaxial needle with the help of pressure monitoring line (150 cm, 1.4 ml) and dropped into the 0.1 M CaCl2 bath. The core shell micro-scaffolds containing the encapsulated cells formed in the gelling solution were washed with HBSS containing antibiotic antimycotic solution twice and suspended in DMEM media. They were stored in incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for further culture.

The beads without cells after preparation were washed with HBSS buffer (prepared in autoclaved water followed by filter sterilization) and placed in liquid nitrogen for snap freeze. They were then crushed and lyophilized in Labconco's FreeZone freeze dryer to observe their pore size. The lyophilized beads were then mounted on sample holder using double sided carbon tape, gold sputtered and then imaged using Zeiss Evo 18 SEM system.

The viability of encapsulated cells (A549, MG-63, MDA-MB231, U2OS and L929) in both core and shell regions were assessed by live dead staining. After culturing the cells for 48/96 h, the media was taken out and the gels were stained by addition of DMEM containing Calcein AM (2 μM) and propidium iodide (2 μM). The microbeads were incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. They were washed with HBSS to remove any residual stain. Gels were then observed under Olympus IX 73 fluorescence microscope to evaluate the viability of the cells. The FITC filter was used for Calcein AM and TRITC filter was used for propidium iodide imaging.

Proliferation of the encapsulated cells (A549, MG-63, MDA-MB231, U2OS and L929) was monitored by two methods. One method was the standard protocol of monitoring the metabolic activity of the cells using alamar blue over a period of time. The second method was to monitor change in pH using pH sensitive carbon dots.

Alamar blue (1 mg ml−1) was dissolved in HBSS, filtered through 22 μm filter and diluted 10 times in complete media (DMEM with FBS) with CaCl2 (5 mM) for use. Equal number of cell containing beads were kept in triplicates in 24 well plate and incubated with complete media. The diluted alamar blue was added to the gels (300 μl/well) and incubated at 37 °C. After, 1 h, 100 μl was taken in duplicates in 96 black well plate for all the 3 wells and fluorescence emission at 590 nm was evaluated with excitation of 560 nm using microplate reader. The beads were then washed twice with HBSS followed by complete media. Fresh media was added to the gels at they were incubated in the CO2 incubator for further time points. Alamar blue diluted with complete media served as the negative control. The fluorescence intensities of the samples were normalized with the control. The experiment was carried out three times and each sample in duplicates for the assay.

1% (w/v) alginate and 3% (w/v) gelatin powder were dissolved in the 0.5 mg ml−1 carbon dot solution. Cell suspension was added to this core solution, to form core encapsulating the cells along with the carbon dots. The core shell micro-scaffolds obtained were then incubated in HBSS with antibacterial antimycotic and 10% FBS, to prevent any buffering activity in the CO2 incubator. Equal number of gels in duplicates was taken in 96 well plate, to study the emission at 450 nm and 550 nm with excitation at 365 nm and 500 nm respectively. The experiment was carried out three times and each sample in duplicates for the assay.

The encapsulated cells (MG-63 and HuH-7) from the alginate gelatin beads were extracted using RNAse free 55 mM sodium citrate solution. Total RNA from extracted cells were isolated using TRIzol method following manufacturer's protocol. RNA (1 μg per 20 μl reaction volume) was reverse transcribed into cDNA using iScript™ cDNA Synthesis Kit according to manufacturer's instructions. Gene expression was measured using real-time quantitative PCR on the CFX96TM Real-Time PCR detection system (Bio Rad). PCR was performed using SsoFast™ Eva Green® Supermix according to the manufacturer's specifications. The gene expression was normalized to GAPDH as internal control gene for EMT genes and RUNX2. 18 s rRNA was used as internal control for hepatic progenitor marker as per the availability of the primers. The expressions determined from reactions run in triplicate on the same plate. For each gene, fold change relative to the control (cells grown on tissue culture plate) was calculated using the ΔΔCt method. Error bars represent the SD of the ΔΔCt values.
All quantitative data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance is accepted as p < 0.05.

Liver specific functions of the hepatocellular carcinoma (HuH-7) cells cultured in 2D and 3D conditions were studied by measuring 24 h albumin secretion in the cell culture supernatants. Albumin was measured using a commercially available ELISA kit (Sigma Aldrich) designed for the quantitative measurement of albumin levels in cell culture conditioned media.
All quantitative data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance is accepted as p < 0.05.

The cell encapsulated (L929) core shell micro-scaffolds were incubated with Image iT™ Green Hypoxia Reagent for 48/96 h. The micro-scaffolds were washed with HBSS to remove the excess stain. The beads were imaged in Olympus IX 73 microscope under brightfield using 4X objective.

Cells (MG-63) were encapsulated in both shell and core. Doxorubicin (2 μM) was administered for 30 min in the experimental setup. The concentration was decided in the range commonly used for cytotoxicity assay for cell lines. The concentration reported showed maximum efficient uptake with less cytotoxicity in the short duration of 30 min. It was replaced with fresh media after 30 min and further incubated for 4 h. Imaging was done after 4 h using Olympus Fluoview FV1000 confocal microscope using a 10× objective. The cells were stained with Calcein AM to show live cells after doxorubicin administration.

104 cells (MG-63) were encapsulated in core-shell micro-scaffold, and in only core architecture. The same number of cells were plated on 96 well tissue culture plate. The cells in both the micro-scaffolds and tissue culture plates were given 2 μM Doxorubicin treatment for 30 min. They were replaced with fresh media and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Alamar blue assay was performed and the fluorescence was recorded at 575 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing and characterization of core-shell micro-scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para:
We developed an in vitro 3D tumor culture platform by designing alginate based micro-scaffold with core shell architecture using a simple droplet microfluidic technique [15,16]. The core shell micro-scaffold architecture was created with the help of a coaxial needle attached in a droplet microfluidic setup as illustrated in Fig. 1
a. Over the years, the biomaterial of choice for encapsulated cells in a droplet has been alginate, which is an FDA approved biocompatible polysaccharide. Apart from its biocompatibility it is also the material of choice because of its potential to crosslink in presence of calcium ions. Alginate based systems also aid in retrieving the cells by using sodium citrate that degrades the scaffold, which allows further studies to be carried out [17,18]. Encapsulated cells need to adhere to substratum to proliferate. Unfortunately, alginate itself does not have cell-adhesion properties, because of its lack of interaction with integrins. To enhance its adhesive ability and to mimic the extracellular matrix (ECM) better, alginate is complemented with gelatin, instead of cell specific and expensive peptides like IKVAV, RGD etc. [17]. The crosslinking of the scaffolds was done through ion exchange process using calcium chloride. Alginate with 2% gelatin for shell and 3% gelatin for core was used to prepare the 3D micro-scaffolds. Gelatin percentage for core and shell was optimized for achieving the proper core shell structure. The core density needed to be kept higher as compared the shell to attain the proper core shell architecture. The architectural control was further achieved by introducing PEGDA to the system, which allows controlling the crosslinking density of the core or the shell, thus mimicking the scaffold properties of various tumor grades. The incorporation of PEGDA also allows dual crosslinking and improves the mechanical property of the scaffold. The PEGDA concentration range used was from 3% to 8%. The lower range showed effective change in crosslinking as compared to without PEGDA in the system. Concentration higher than 8% was observed to be detrimental to the mammalian cells owing to higher UV exposure required for crosslinking. Using this method, we were able to fabricate a large number (approximately 500) of monodispersed core-shell droplets with uniform of size 400 μm to 500 μm in 1 min (Fig. S1). The chemical composition of the micro-scaffolds core and shell were separately analyzed by FTIR as shown in Fig. 1 b, showing that gelatin and alginate maintained their individual chemical properties. The pore size of the scaffold containing 1% alginate and 3% gelatin observed by SEM were 20 μm in average Fig. 1 c, which is enough for the exchange of nutrients and can also facilitate cellular migration in presence of biological cues. The core shell architecture of the micro-scaffold can be clearly observed in Fig. 1 d by the optical image captured immediately after the synthesis of the beads. In addition, the porosity can be controlled artificially by changing the shell composition with PEGDA. It was observed that by changing the percentage of PEGDA from 3% to 8% in the shell, we obtained a shrink of 7.6% in the gel size. The change in crosslinking densities was confirmed by SEM images Fig. 1 e, which clearly indicates smaller pore sizes in gel containing 8% PEGDA in the shell. This control over crosslinking density allows us to achieve a platform in which the pore sizes can be artificially altered for studies requiring varied crosslinking densities of the ECM. It can be used to develop more in vivo like tumor spheroids where achieving the highly strenuous ECM with high cell density requires a long culture time in vitro. After confirming the control over composition, size, structural core shell architecture and porosity of the micro-scaffolds, we studied if this methodology can be utilized for selectively encapsulating cells in region specific manner. For this, we used two differently labeled mammalian osteosarcoma cells MG-63, one with green cell tracker dye 5-chloromethylfluorescein diacetate (CMFDA) and other with red cell tracker dye, CM Dil, for encapsulating cells in the core and shell respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 2 the cells of the core and shell were spatially separate but present in close proximity, clearly indicating compartmentalization of cells. This arrangement can enable studies that can provide insight into various cellular interactions and highlight the importance of specific cell signaling. We also confirmed that along with encapsulation of cells in specific region the micro-scaffold also provided it the environment for growth and proliferation. For future studies, the micro-scaffold can be modified according to the needs of a particular cell type to increase its cellular compatibility, proliferation and differentiation potential. We can change the polymer composition, their crosslinking density resulting in altered pore size as required by the cells. One of the many combinations that can be utilized to make it more similar to the in vivo composition can be use of decellularized matrix. In this similar protocol the matrix can be partitioned according to the two types of cells encapsulated to elicit specific response against matrix composition.

As can be seen from the live dead assay studies in Fig. S2, encapsulated cells were proliferating and alive (indicated by fluorescent green Calcein AM dye) both in core and in shell, tracking dyes are retained in the cells even after multiple generations and allow us to easily monitor localization of cells. With the ability to encapsulate different cell types in spatially separate regions in micron scale, we proceeded to validate the platform for studying growth of cells. Interestingly, we found that not many methods are available to study or monitor the growth rate of cells. The most commonly used methods such as MTT, XTT, and Alamar Blue assay are mostly end point based thus, hindering the continuous monitoring of growth over long periods after encapsulation. To overcome this, we utilized a previously reported pH sensitive carbon dot based nanoprobes for studying the growth rate of various encapsulated cells in our 3D micro-scaffold platform [19]. For this, the carbon dots were encapsulated along with cells in the droplet microfluidic process for obtaining micro-scaffolds with carbon dots and cells together. The carbon dots (CDs) have an intrinsic fluorescence in blue (excitation- 365 nm; emission- 450 nm) and green (excitation- 500 nm; emission- 550 nm) region and it has been reported that while blue florescence remains constant the green fluorescence increases as the pH of the medium decreases, thus making it a perfect probe for ratiometric detection of change in pH [20]. Interestingly, decrease in pH corresponds to the hyperplasic growth of cancer cells and can thus be used for monitoring growth rates. For comparison of the growth rate a standard Alamar Blue assay was also performed for cells growing in the micro-scaffolds over 5 days. The study was done using various mammalian cancer cell lines with different doubling times, as reported in literature from growth studies done in 2D tissue culture plates. The cells chosen were, L929 (fibroblast, doubling time = 14 h), MDA-MB231 (highly aggressive breast cancer cell line, doubling times = 36 h), U-2 OS (osteosarcoma, doubling time = 29 h) and A549 (adenocarcinoma epithelial, doubling time = 20 h). The cells were encapsulated into the micro-scaffolds and the proliferation rates were monitored by recording the blue and the green fluorescence intensities. In parallel, the growth rates were also monitored with standard alamar blue assay for the same duration of 5 days. The growth curves obtained by the two methods were concordant with each other as shown in Fig. 3
a. Thus, it affirms that the 3D micro-scaffold platform developed allows an easy monitoring of cell growth and the cell growth is not altered due to encapsulation in the 3D alginate based micro-scaffolds. The proliferation profile and cell viability with MG-63 cells encapsulated in alginate-gelatin micro-scaffold with 3% and 8% PEGDA in the shell solution was also performed. The results are shown in supplementary figures (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4) and no significant difference was observed in the growth of cells encapsulated in the core with varying shell crosslinking density. Further, the proliferation profile of cell lines of the same lineage (osteosarcoma) was compared to study if this system can be used to differentiate cells based on their growth rates. It is noted in the literature that the proliferation rate can also be indicative of the high invasive potential of cells [21]. The U-2 OS and MG-63 cells are both osteosarcoma cell lines but with varied proliferation rates [22]. U-2 OS has high invasive potential as compared to MG-63 [23]. As can be seen in Fig. 3 b, a clear difference in growth curve of MG-63 and U-2 OS was noticed. It was observed that there is an increase in proliferation in MG-63 as compared to U2OS in the initial days. However, as the growth progresses the MG63 cell line tends to stabilize, whereas U2OS proliferation rate increases. This states that U-2 OS cells have a faster growth compared to the MG-63 suggesting its highly invasive and motile characteristic compared to a less invasive and stationary cell line MG-63. This observation also highlights the importance of our platform in conducting growth rate studies over longer duration by avoiding cumbersome assays. Since, many factors such as cell-cell proximity, cell cycle phases, cell-ECM cues etc. can affect the growth of cells, early time point growth curves may be illusive and may not be the right for predicting the growth profile of cells. Thus, there is an urgent need for methods that can eliminate the use of cumbersome cell growth assays and provide simple readouts for monitoring cell growth over weeks for more precise prediction of cellular growth profile.

Excited about the potential of the platform to monitor growth rates of encapsulated cells, we progressed to establish if a hypoxic core can be generated quickly without waiting for increasing the cell density as typically done in standard 3D cultures. Tumor architecture consists of heterogeneous population of cells. They are composed of a necrotic core, quiescent or viable middle zone followed by a proliferating outer zone. To create a realistic tumor model it is essential that the models have unequal supply of nutrients as it is in the in vivo scenario. In a standard macro 3D culture of tumors or spheroid generation, it usually becomes dense at the core and starts limiting diffusion of nutrients in about 14 to 21 days [24]. To study if a hypoxic region can be generated in the core of the micro-scaffold faster due to the small volume, we treated the encapsulated cells with hypoxia detecting dye, Hypoxia IT green dye. The dye gives a green fluorescence in low oxygen levels, signifying hypoxia in that region. As discussed earlier the crosslinking density of the shell can be controlled by altering the PEGDA content and hence the constraint microenvironment can be created faster. As can be seen from Fig. 3 c, the core had hypoxic regions that increased significantly over a period of 96 h. It was interesting to note that while the core fluorescence increased over time, there were some cells in the periphery, which did not fluoresce suggesting the radial hypoxia condition, which is also observed in real tumors. This study suggested that there is an unequal supply of nutrients and oxygen, and also indicated that the cells encapsulated are a heterogeneous population of cells, with a hypoxic core and non-hypoxic region in the periphery of the core, which is very similar to an in vivo tumor architecture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Genetic expression and functionality of encapsulated cells
ce:section.ce:para:
After confirming the functionality of our platform for real time monitoring of cell growth, we proceeded to study if the 3D environment offers an advantage over cells growing on 2D. For this, we first evaluated the effect on genetic expressions and functionality of cells that are more sensitive to niche environment such as hepatic cells. A platform that can preserve the functions of hepatocytes are essential for developing 3D culture systems for studying hepatic carcinoma. For this, hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (Huh-7) was grown as a monolayer on conducive 2D and in the developed alginate 3D micro-scaffolds. The gene expression analysis was performed to assess the expression of EpCAM and CD133, which are hepatic progenitor cell markers, in the cells encapsulated in alginate micro-scaffold for 21 days. For CD133, there was no significant change in its expression between cells in 2D and 3D alginate micro-scaffolds. In case of EpCAM, there was about 2 ± 0.2 fold (p < 0.05) upregulation in cells cultured in the micro-scaffold as compared to cells in 2D condition. This is indicative of the fact that 3D culture of cells in the alginate micro scaffold promotes the expression of stemness markers. Hepatic EpCAM is a marker for most epithelial cancers and also hepatic progenitor cells (Fig. 4
b). With respect to hepatic functions, we measured the levels of secreted albumin in the supernatants of the cells cultured in 3D for 21 days. In comparison to 2D, albumin production was enhanced in cells cultured in the 3D alginate micro-scaffolds (2 fold, p < 0.05), suggesting that the 3D alginate micro-scaffolds provide the most conducive environment to support enhanced functions of the hepatoma cells (Fig. 4 a). We also evaluated if the same can be observed for other cancer cell types as it has been shown in the literature that 3D encapsulated cells show better genetic expression as compared to 2D. We evaluated the genetic expressions by qPCR analysis of various genes in the micro-scaffold encapsulated MG-63 cells (Fig. 4 c). The genes include EMT markers (Twist, N cadherin, E cadherin) and osteosarcoma characteristic marker (RUNX2). MG-63 is a less migratory cell line owing to higher expression of E cadherin gene. Twist and N cadherin are mesenchymal markers, which are overexpressed in a migratory phenotype. Lower expression in MG-63 shows that the characteristics are maintained. RUNX2 expression also confirms that MG-63 is unaltered in its marking features. Apart, from maintaining the genotype we also observe significantly better expression of the above genes in 3D micro-scaffold as compared to cell grown in tissue culture plate (p < 0.05). Thus, the platform can not only be used to monitor the growth of cancer cells in real time without carrying out lengthy end point assays, but the growth rates can also be utilized for preliminary prediction of aggressiveness of the encapsulated cells with a reference cell line.

Intrigued by the hypoxic region in the core, we progressed into evaluating if there exists a differential diffusion of drugs in the micro-scaffold. It is important to note that while 2D platforms offer uniform diffusion of drugs, in a real tumor, there is poor diffusion of the drugs near the core, thus making the core almost inaccessible to any chemotherapeutic. This simple discrepancy observed between 2D and real tumors is one of the reasons for the incoherence observed between the results of 2D and in vivo drug efficacy studies [16]. Considering the potential of the developed platform for drug screening studies, we explored if the differential diffusion of a chemotherapeutic, Doxorubicin (Dox), is observed in the core shell micro-scaffolds. For this we treated the cell-laden micro-scaffolds with doxorubicin for 30 min followed by replacement of the solution with fresh media and culturing the cells for 4 h. The live cells were then observed via confocal microscopy after staining with live cell staining dye Calcein AM. As can be seen from the images in Fig. 5
a, more cells were alive in the shell when no Dox treatment was done compared to lesser green cells in shell with Dox treatment. In contrast, the cell density was similar and unaltered in the core irrespective of the treatment. This confirmed that while Dox uptake was higher in the cells in shell. Due to quick diffusion it was not able to penetrate through the core adequately and hence the affect in core cells was considerably less. This observation of low Dox diffusion in the core-shell 3D architecture clearly demonstrates that this system can be used for drug screening and represents better model than a 2D tissue culture. Thus, to validate the potential of the developed 3D micro-scaffold in studying the efficacy of Dox, we quantified the viable cells after exposure to same concentration of Dox when cultured in the developed 3D micro-scaffold, on 2D tissue culture plate, only core 3D micro-scaffold and on collagen core scaffold, which is typically used as for 3D culturing of cells (Fig. 5 b). After 24 h of cell culture the viable cells on the various platforms were quantified by alamar blue assay. It was observed that while the viability of the cells on 2D tissue culture plates was lowest, the core micro-scaffolds had slightly higher viable cells but not significantly different from the 2D. In comparison, at 2 μM Dox concentration studied, the 3D core-shell micro-scaffold had least cell death due to Dox exposure and most of the cells were viable. Thus, indicating that the core shell architecture would require more Dox concentration to achieve similar viabilities. Interestingly, both only core 3D platforms, the micro-scaffold and collagen bead, did not offer diffusion barriers similar to that observed in in vivo tumors. Alamar blue assay for a range of Dox concentrations was also performed to compare 2D vs the 3D core-shell scaffold and to further validate the result observed (Fig. S5). As seen before, in comparison, the same concentration of Dox resulted in least decrease in cell viability for cells encapsulated in core-shell architecture micro-scaffolds. This states the fact that slightly higher drug concentration is required to achieve effective cell death in 3D condition and an effective drug concentration obtained by studies in a 2D culture may not result in similar effects in vivo. In fact, it is often observed that in the in vivo conditions the drugs become ineffective in the same dose as the 2D results, thus leading to their failure in the clinical trials. Thus, this study strongly validates the developed core shell architecture 3D micro-scaffold platform to be better in comparison to the tumor spheroids for drug screening studies.

In conclusion, the above developed alginate based platform has the ability to spatially separate two kinds of cells simultaneously while maintaining a close proximity which can be utilized to understand the intercellular interactions in 3D environment. Proliferation study using carbon based nano sensors gives the benefit of monitoring changes in pH in real time, which correlates with the growth profile, thus omitting the need of end-point assays for studying cellular growth. The 3D environment supports and enhances the genetic expression of the cell specific markers. It also shows improved functioning of the cells as observed by the better albumin production in HuH-7 cells as compared to monolayer culture. The platform can be used for drug screening as it truly mimics the heterogeneous tumor architecture and generates hypoxic cores within a short span of 96 h compared to 14 to 21 days by traditional methods. In addition, the constraint microenvironment as observed in tumor ECM is also confirmed by poor diffusion of drugs to the core. Thus, this platform takes us a step closer to 3D platforms with real time pH monitoring as in vitro models for drug screening.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from Indian Institute of Technology Delhi. We are grateful to CRF, IITD for SEM facility and NRF, IITD for FTIR and CBME, IITD for RTPCR facility. We acknowledge Dr. Mayank Singh's lab for gifting us the cell line U2OS. We also acknowledge Prof. Harpal Sir's lab and Ms. Neha, CBME for confocal imaging. We acknowledge IITD FIRP project (MI0226) sponsored by MHRD, Govt. of India and IRD, IITD.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2021.112344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro tumor models: advantages, disadvantages, variables, and selecting the right platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.E. Katt, A.L. Placone, A.D. Wong, Z.S. Xu, P.C. Searson, In Vitro Tumor Models: Advantages, Disadvantages, Variables, and Selecting the Right Platform, Frontiers in bioengineering and biotechnology 4 (2016) 12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three-dimensional in vitro cell culture models in drug discovery and drug repositioning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Langhans, Three-Dimensional in Vitro Cell Culture Models in Drug Discovery and Drug Repositioning, Frontiers in pharmacology 9 (2018) 6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The third dimension bridges the gap between cell culture and live tissue, nature reviews
